skillName: Identify and name types of angles
misconceptionSummary: Students identify angles by the length of the arms rather than the amount of turn between them.

visuals.ruleText: An angle measures the amount of turn between two lines — not the length of those lines
visuals.subText: Long arms and short arms at the same opening make the same angle

steps.narration: An angle is a measure of rotation — how much one line has turned from another. The length of the arms is irrelevant. A very short opening and a very wide opening are what create different angle types, not the size of the lines themselves.

lines.text: Clock hand from 12 to 3 → quarter turn → 90° right angle
Clock hand from 12 to 6 → half turn → 180° straight line
Clock hand from 12 past 6 back toward 12 → more than half → reflex

steps.narration: Think of a clock hand sweeping from twelve. To three is a quarter turn — a right angle. To six is a half turn — a straight line at one hundred and eighty degrees. Continuing past six toward twelve again is more than a half turn — a reflex angle.

lines.text: Two angles both opening to 45° — same angle type
One has arms 1 cm long, the other 10 cm long
Both are acute — arm length changes nothing  ✓

steps.narration: Two angles both open to forty-five degrees — one with short arms, one with long arms. They are both acute. The arm length tells you nothing about the angle type. Only the amount of rotation matters.

visuals.expression: Angle type = amount of turn, not arm length
visuals.label: Quarter turn = right (90°).  Half turn = straight (180°).  More = reflex.

steps.narration: Angle type is determined entirely by the rotation between the two arms. A quarter turn is a right angle, a half turn is a straight line, and more than that is reflex. Arm length plays no part.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking a longer arm makes a bigger angle. Arm length is irrelevant — only the opening between the arms determines the angle type. A tiny gap between long arms can still be acute.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Angles measure rotation, not length. Two angles with the same opening are the same type regardless of how long their arms are. Focus on the gap between the arms — that is the angle.
